disclaimer: VALIDATED action/effect library (v1). Each entry's effect size, evidence grade, real-outcome target, and PMID were extracted from primary literature and independently audited; QALY conversions (qaly_per_event, burden_qaly) are calibrated, internally-consistent estimates. Lifestyle entries carry per-unit baseline-dependent dose_response and POTENTIAL QALYs (no achievability discount; achievability handled at selection). Surrogate-anchored entries are surrogate_flag=true and graded weak. Acute reactive levers (primary PCI, thrombectomy) are intentionally out of scope. Still illustrative; verify before clinical use.

actions.target_outcome: smoking-attributable mortality and disease burden OUTSIDE the lipid/BP CVD pathways (lung cancer + COPD + non-CVD cancers + residual CV death) plus the CKD-progression slice: composite of >=50% eGFR reduction, dialysis initiation, or kidney transplantation in an active smoker

actions.target_outcome: progression to kidney failure / sustained >=50% eGFR decline / ESKD / renal or CV death, in rapid eGFR progressors (the subgroup where ~96% of composite kidney events arise)
actions.source: Heerspink HJL, Stefánsson BV, Correa-Rotter R, et al. Dapagliflozin in Patients with Chronic Kidney Disease (DAPA-CKD). N Engl J Med. 2020;383(15):1436-1446. Primary composite (sustained >=50% eGFR decline / ESKD / renal or CV death) HR 0.61 (95% CI 0.51-0.72). Prespecified slope analysis: 368/385 (96%) composite kidney endpoints occurred in fast progressors (>5 mL/min/1.73m2/yr), placing this hard-outcome benefit squarely in the rapid-progressor subgroup. Corroborated by the SMART-C/Nuffield pooled meta-analysis (Lancet 2022;400:1788-1801, PMID 36351458): kidney-disease-progression RR 0.63 (95% CI 0.58-0.69). https://www.nejm.org/doi/full/10.1056/NEJMoa2024816

actions.target_outcome: Slowing kidney-function decline / delaying ESRD in ADPKD. Proven outcome is surrogate-anchored: TEMPO 3:4 primary endpoint was total-kidney-volume growth rate; the supporting effect (HR 0.87) is a composite ADPKD clinical-progression endpoint (worsening kidney function defined by sustained reciprocal-creatinine decline, kidney pain, hypertension, albuminuria), not a hard ESKD/dialysis/doubling-of-creatinine endpoint.
actions.source: Torres VE, Chapman AB, Devuyst O, et al. Tolvaptan in patients with autosomal dominant polycystic kidney disease (TEMPO 3:4). N Engl J Med. 2012;367(25):2407-2418. https://www.nejm.org/doi/full/10.1056/NEJMoa1205511

actions.target_outcome: slowing chronic eGFR decline / delaying progression to kidney failure in IgA nephropathy (and FSGS) -- surrogate-anchored (eGFR slope, not a hard endpoint)
actions.source: Rovin BH, Barratt J, Heerspink HJL, et al. Efficacy and safety of sparsentan versus irbesartan in patients with IgA nephropathy (PROTECT): 2-year results from a randomised, active-controlled, phase 3 trial. Lancet. 2023 Dec 2;402(10417):2077-2090. doi:10.1016/S0140-6736(23)02302-4. PROTECT (dual endothelin/angiotensin receptor antagonist vs maximally-titrated irbesartan, N=404, biopsy-proven IgAN): chronic eGFR slope (wk6-110) -2.7 vs -3.8 mL/min/1.73m2/yr, difference +1.1 (95% CI 0.1-2.1, p=0.037); total slope (d1-110) difference +1.0 (95% CI -0.03 to 1.94, p=0.058, MISSED). UPCR reduction -42.8% vs -4.4%. FSGS sibling trial DUPLEX missed its total-slope endpoint. Endpoint is eGFR-slope surrogate, NOT a hard kidney-failure outcome -> surrogate_flag=true, graded weak per project surrogate rule. https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/37931634/

actions.source: Järvinen HJ, Aarnio M, Mustonen H, Aktan-Collan K, Aaltonen LA, Peltomäki P, de la Chapelle A, Mecklin JP. Controlled 15-year trial on screening for colorectal cancer in families with hereditary nonpolyposis colorectal cancer. Gastroenterology. 2000 May;118(5):829-34. https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/10784581/

actions.target_outcome: colorectal cancer incidence in Lynch syndrome (MMR-deficiency) carriers
actions.source: Burn J, Sheth H, Elliott F, et al. Cancer prevention with aspirin in hereditary colorectal cancer (Lynch syndrome), 10-year follow-up and registry-based 20-year data in the CAPP2 study: a double-blind, randomised, placebo-controlled trial. Lancet. 2020 Jun 13;395(10240):1855-1863. https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/32534647/

actions.source: Schmeler KM, Lynch HT, Chen LM, et al. Prophylactic surgery to reduce the risk of gynecologic cancers in the Lynch syndrome. N Engl J Med. 2006;354(3):261-269. https://www.nejm.org/doi/full/10.1056/NEJMoa052627

actions.source: Nielsen M, van Steenbergen LN, Jones N, et al. Survival of MUTYH-associated polyposis patients with colorectal cancer and matched control colorectal cancer patients. J Natl Cancer Inst. 2010;102(22):1724-30. https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/21044966/

actions.target_outcome: all-cancer mortality / overall survival in germline TP53 carriers (Li-Fraumeni syndrome) via comprehensive multi-organ surveillance (Toronto protocol: whole-body MRI + brain MRI + breast MRI/mammography + abdo-pelvic ultrasound + colonoscopy + biochemical screening)
actions.source: Villani A, Shore A, Wasserman JD, et al. Biochemical and imaging surveillance in germline TP53 mutation carriers with Li-Fraumeni syndrome: 11 year follow-up of a prospective observational study. Lancet Oncol. 2016;17(9):1295-1305. https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/27501770/

actions.target_outcome: syndrome-specific cancer incidence/mortality in high-penetrance hereditary cancer syndromes (CDH1 prophylactic total gastrectomy, FAP colectomy, MEN2/RET thyroidectomy, PALB2 breast MRI) via risk-reducing surgery or intensified surveillance
actions.source: Stillman MD, Kusche N, Toledano S, et al. Short and Long-Term Outcomes of Prophylactic Total Gastrectomy in 54 Consecutive Individuals with Germline Pathogenic Mutations in the CDH1 Gene. J Surg Oncol. 2022;126(8):1413-1422. doi:10.1002/jso.27084 https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9649870/

actions.target_outcome: mortality for otherwise-unscreened lethal cancers via stage shift (cfDNA multi-cancer early detection signal -> diagnostic workup -> earlier-stage curative treatment)
actions.source: Schrag D, Beer TM, McDonnell CH 3rd, et al. Blood-based tests for multicancer early detection (PATHFINDER): a prospective cohort study. Lancet. 2023 Oct 7;402(10409):1251-1260. https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/37805216/

actions.target_outcome: incident probable dementia / MCI (adjudicated composite)
actions.source: Reboussin DM, Gaussoin SA, Pajewski NM, et al. Long-Term Effect of Intensive vs Standard Blood Pressure Control on Mild Cognitive Impairment and Probable Dementia in SPRINT. Neurology. 2025;104(3):e213334. doi:10.1212/WNL.0000000000213334 https://www.neurology.org/doi/10.1212/WNL.0000000000213334

actions.target_outcome: 3-year cognitive change (global cognition standardized composite score) in older adults with hearing loss at high risk of cognitive decline; ARIC high-risk subgroup
actions.source: Lin FR, Pike JR, Albert MS, et al. Hearing intervention versus health education control to reduce cognitive decline in older adults with hearing loss in the USA (ACHIEVE): a multicentre, randomised controlled trial. Lancet. 2023 Sep 2;402(10404):786-797. https://www.thelancet.com/journals/lancet/article/PIIS0140-6736(23)01406-X/abstract

actions.target_outcome: cognitive function (reversal/non-progression of deficiency-related cognitive impairment via B12/folate repletion and thyroid replacement)
actions.source: Li S, Guo Y, Men J, Fu H, Xu T. The preventive efficacy of vitamin B supplements on the cognitive decline of elderly adults: a systematic review and meta-analysis. BMC Geriatr. 2021;21(1):367. https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/34134667/

actions.target_outcome: Clinical progression of early Alzheimer measured by CDR-SB slowing -- for asymptomatic/preclinical (screening-detected, amyloid-positive) use. Distinct from the symptomatic indication (act_anti_amyloid_mab_symptomatic).
actions.source: van Dyck CH, Swanson CJ, Aisen P, et al. Lecanemab in Early Alzheimer's Disease. N Engl J Med. 2023;388(1):9-21. (CLARITY AD; CDR-SB change 1.21 lecanemab vs 1.66 placebo at 18 mo, difference -0.45, 95% CI -0.67 to -0.23, i.e. 27% relative slowing; ARIA-E 12.6%, ARIA-H 17.3%.) Asymptomatic/preclinical indication (AHEAD 3-45, NCT04468659) remains unreported; effect here is extrapolated from this symptomatic trial. https://www.nejm.org/doi/full/10.1056/NEJMoa2212948

actions.target_outcome: clinical progression of early symptomatic Alzheimer disease (CDR-SB decline) in amyloid-positive MCI/mild dementia
actions.source: van Dyck CH, Swanson CJ, Aisen P, et al. Lecanemab in Early Alzheimer's Disease (CLARITY-AD). N Engl J Med. 2023 Jan 5;388(1):9-21. https://www.nejm.org/doi/10.1056/NEJMoa2212948

actions.target_outcome: ipsilateral / any ischemic stroke in asymptomatic severe (>=70%) carotid stenosis
actions.source: Halliday A, et al.; Asymptomatic Carotid Surgery Trial (ACST) Collaborative Group. 10-year stroke prevention after successful carotid endarterectomy for asymptomatic stenosis (ACST-1): a multicentre randomised trial. Lancet. 2010 Sep 25;376(9746):1074-84. https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/20870099/

actions.target_outcome: incident ischemic stroke per 1.0 mmol/L LDL reduction (cerebrovascular slice; coronary/MACE stock stays in CVD)
actions.source: Cholesterol Treatment Trialists' (CTT) Collaboration. Efficacy and safety of more intensive lowering of LDL cholesterol: a meta-analysis of data from 170,000 participants in 26 randomised trials. Lancet 2010;376(9753):1670-81. Ischaemic stroke RR 0.79 (95% CI 0.74-0.85, p<0.0001) per 1.0 mmol/L LDL reduction; non-significant excess of haemorrhagic stroke RR 1.12 (0.93-1.35). Corroborated by SPARCL (Amarenco, NEJM 2006;355:549-59, PMID 16899775): atorvastatin 80mg cut fatal/nonfatal stroke ~16% in recent stroke/TIA. https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/21067804/

actions.target_outcome: disease-specific quality of life and motor disability (PDQ-39 summary index, UPDRS-III) in Parkinson's disease with early/established motor complications
actions.source: Schuepbach WMM, Rau J, Knudsen K, et al; EARLYSTIM Study Group. Neurostimulation for Parkinson's Disease with Early Motor Complications. N Engl J Med. 2013;368(7):610-622. https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/23406026/

actions.target_outcome: confirmed disability progression (sustained EDSS worsening) and relapse rate in relapsing/active MS
actions.source: Hauser SL, Bar-Or A, Comi G, et al. Ocrelizumab versus Interferon Beta-1a in Relapsing Multiple Sclerosis (OPERA I and OPERA II). N Engl J Med. 2017;376(3):221-234. https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/28002679/

actions.target_outcome: gait/mobility disability and falls in idiopathic NPH responsive to temporary CSF drainage (CSF shunt vs placebo valve)
actions.source: Luciano MG, Williams MA, Hamilton MG, et al. A Randomized Trial of Shunting for Idiopathic Normal-Pressure Hydrocephalus (PENS trial). N Engl J Med. 2025;393(22):2198-2209. doi:10.1056/NEJMoa2503109 https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/40960253/

actions.target_outcome: incident dementia (new dementia diagnosis over 7-year follow-up)
actions.source: Eyting M, Xie M, Michalik F, Heß S, Chung S, Geldsetzer P. A natural experiment on the effect of herpes zoster vaccination on dementia. Nature. 2025;641(8062):438-446. https://doi.org/10.1038/s41586-025-08800-x

actions.target_outcome: incident all-cause dementia (and Alzheimer dementia) in older adults with vision-impairing cataract
actions.source: Lee CS, Gibbons LE, Lee AY, et al. Association Between Cataract Extraction and Development of Dementia. JAMA Intern Med. 2022;182(2):134-141. https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC8649913/

actions.target_outcome: alcohol-attributable morbidity/mortality OUTSIDE the ASCVD/BP pathways -- atrial fibrillation, hemorrhagic and ischemic stroke, alcohol-associated liver disease, and alcohol-attributable cancers (oral, pharyngeal, esophageal SCC, liver, female breast, colorectal)
actions.source: Voskoboinik A, et al. Alcohol Abstinence in Drinkers with Atrial Fibrillation. N Engl J Med. 2020;382(1):20-28. (AF recurrence HR 0.55 with abstinence RCT). Dose-response support: Millwood IY, et al. Conventional and genetic evidence on alcohol and vascular disease aetiology: a prospective study of 500,000 men and women in China. Lancet. 2019;393(10183):1831-1842 (PMID 30955975; MR: ~12% higher stroke per 1-2 drinks/day, ~35% per 4 drinks/day, monotonic, no protective J-curve). Cancer dose-response: Bagnardi V, et al. Alcohol consumption and site-specific cancer risk: a comprehensive dose-response meta-analysis. Br J Cancer. 2014;112(3):580-593 (PMID 25422909). IARC Monographs/Handbooks Vol 20A (sufficient evidence that reduction lowers oral/esophageal cancer); USPSTF 2018 Grade B alcohol screening + brief intervention. https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/31893513/

dose_response.unit: per -1 standard drink/day (~ -10 to -12.5 g ethanol/day)
dose_response.reference_baseline: lifetime abstention / zero intake as the risk-minimizing anchor (the never-vs-moderate J-curve is non-causal confounding per Mendelian randomization, Millwood Lancet 2019)
dose_response.baseline_dependence: Monotonic from the first drink for cancer and stroke -- no protective floor, no safe threshold. RELATIVE per-drink slope is roughly constant (log-linear) across the range, but ABSOLUTE QALY return per drink removed is steepest at the heavy end where AF burden, alcohol-associated liver disease, and hemorrhagic-stroke risk concentrate (AF-abstinence RCT benefit HR 0.55 was in drinkers averaging ~17 drinks/wk). Light-to-moderate reductions still earn credit via the cancer slice (esophageal SCC RR ~1.26-1.30 at <=12.5 g/day per Bagnardi 2015) but per-unit absolute gain is smaller there.

actions.target_outcome: Major cardiovascular events (MI, stroke, CV death) in high-CV-risk primary prevention (PREDIMED hard endpoint); cross-domain dietary-pattern lever also lowering incident type-2 diabetes, diet-sensitive cancer incidence (colorectal/breast), and cognitive decline. Hard outcomes throughout; best-net not additive with statins/activity on the shared ASCVD pathway.
actions.source: Estruch R, Ros E, Salas-Salvado J, et al. Primary Prevention of Cardiovascular Disease with a Mediterranean Diet Supplemented with Extra-Virgin Olive Oil or Nuts. N Engl J Med. 2018;378(25):e34 (retraction-and-republication of NEJM 2013;368:1279-90). Combined Mediterranean-diet arms vs control HR 0.70 (95% CI 0.55-0.89); EVOO 0.69, nuts 0.72; N=7447, median 4.8 y. Dose-response curve from Sofi F, et al. Mediterranean diet and health status: an updated meta-analysis and a proposal for a literature-based adherence score. Public Health Nutr. 2014;17(12):2769-82 (PMID 24476641): +2-point MDS associated with CVD RR 0.90 (0.87-0.92) and mortality RR 0.92. https://www.nejm.org/doi/full/10.1056/NEJMoa1800389

dose_response.unit: per +2-point Mediterranean-diet adherence score (0-9 MDS / PREDIMED-style adherence points)
dose_response.reference_baseline: poor-quality Western diet at low adherence (MDS ~2-3), the PREDIMED reduced-fat control arm
dose_response.baseline_dependence: Steepest climbing out of a low-adherence Western baseline; each +2 MDS points yields ~10% lower CVD risk (Sofi 2014: RR 0.90, 95% CI 0.87-0.92), with the curve flattening at high adherence so incremental points above the top adherence tertile add little. No meaningful credit for moving within an already-Mediterranean diet.

actions.target_outcome: all-cause and cardiovascular mortality, incident type-2 diabetes, activity-sensitive cancer incidence (colorectal/postmenopausal breast/endometrial), and incident dementia -- the cross-cutting physical-activity VOLUME lever (subsumes the cardiorespiratory-fitness/CRF route as an alternate parameterization of the same exposure)
actions.source: Garcia LMT, Pearce M, Abbas A, et al. Non-occupational physical activity and risk of cardiovascular disease, cancer and mortality outcomes: a dose-response meta-analysis of large prospective studies. Br J Sports Med. 2023;57(15):979-989. (94 cohorts, >30M participants; CV mortality RR 0.71 [0.66-0.77], all-cause mortality RR 0.69 at ~8.75 MET-h/wk / ~150 min/wk vs inactive). Steps dose-response anchor: Paluch AE, et al. Daily steps and all-cause mortality: a meta-analysis of 15 international cohorts. Lancet Public Health. 2022;7(3):e219-e228 (PMID 35247352; Q4 [10,901 steps] vs Q1 [3,553 steps] HR 0.47, plateau 6-8k >=60y / 8-10k <60y). CRF/fitness route: Kodama S, et al. Cardiorespiratory fitness as a quantitative predictor of all-cause mortality and cardiovascular events in healthy men and women: a meta-analysis. JAMA. 2009;301(19):2024-2035 (PMID 19454641; ~11-17% lower all-cause mortality per +1 MET). https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10423495/

dose_response.reference_baseline: sedentary baseline ~3,000-4,000 steps/day (Paluch Q1 median 3,553 steps/day; equivalently ~0 MET-h/wk leisure activity)
dose_response.baseline_dependence: Convex/diminishing-returns: steepest at the low end. In Paluch (Q1-referenced) most benefit accrues by the second quartile (Q2 HR 0.60) and additional volume above the plateau yields little further mortality reduction. Plateau is age-dependent: ~6,000-8,000 steps/day for adults >=60y, ~8,000-10,000 steps/day for adults <60y. The CRF/fitness route is the same exposure re-parameterized: per +1 MET of cardiorespiratory fitness, all-cause mortality RR ~0.87 (Kodama JAMA 2009, PMID 19454641), steepest at low fitness.

actions.target_outcome: incident type-2 diabetes + downstream micro/macrovascular disease
actions.source: Hamman RF, Wing RR, Edelstein SL, et al. Effect of weight loss with lifestyle intervention on risk of diabetes. Diabetes Care. 2006;29(9):2102-2107. (DPP within-trial mediation; whole-program scalar from Knowler/DPPOS, Diabetes 2015/DPP Lancet 2009.) https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/16936160/

dose_response.reference_baseline: overweight/obese prediabetic adult at DPP entry (mean BMI 33.9, IGT + impaired fasting glucose), zero weight change = HR 1.0
dose_response.baseline_dependence: Steepest from high adiposity/high fasting glucose; the lever is real weight loss, not diet/activity per se (Hamman 2006 kg-effect held after adjusting for diet and activity). Convex/diminishing absolute returns as weight normalizes; little additional credit near normal BMI. Activity-only or diet-only effort that does not move weight buys far less than the per-kg curve implies.

actions.target_outcome: incident T2DM (IR phenotype caught before HbA1c crosses the prediabetes threshold)
actions.source: Hamman RF, Wing RR, Edelstein SL, et al. Effect of weight loss with lifestyle intervention on risk of diabetes. Diabetes Care. 2006 Sep;29(9):2102-7. (DPP intensive-lifestyle arm: 16% lower diabetes risk per kg lost; HR 0.42 per 5-kg loss, 95% CI 0.35-0.51.) Causal support: Wainberg M, et al. Homogeneity in the association of body mass index with type 2 diabetes across the UK Biobank: A Mendelian randomization study. PLoS Med. 2019 Dec 10;16(12):e1002982 (genetically-predicted BMI causal for T2DM, OR ~1.25-1.36 per +1 kg/m2, roughly log-linear across the BMI range). https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/16936160/

dose_response.unit: per -1 kg body weight (intensive-lifestyle weight loss)
dose_response.reference_baseline: DPP-type insulin-resistant adult, baseline BMI ~33.9 kg/m2, weight-stable; HR measured per kilogram lost relative to no weight change
dose_response.baseline_dependence: Steepest where adiposity / insulin resistance is highest -- the IR phenotype is the steep part of the curve; each early kilogram buys the most risk reduction. Returns diminish as weight normalizes: little-to-no incremental diabetes-prevention credit once the ~7% / 5-kg lifestyle goal is reached, and effectively no credit below ~BMI 22 where the IR driver is gone.

actions.target_outcome: type-2 diabetes remission (HbA1c <6.5% off all glucose-lowering drugs) + downstream micro/macrovascular disease, via intentional total-body-weight loss in recent-onset T2DM
actions.source: Lean MEJ, Leslie WS, Barnes AC, et al. Primary care-led weight management for remission of type 2 diabetes (DiRECT): an open-label, cluster-randomised trial. Lancet. 2018;391(10120):541-551. (2-year durability: Lean MEJ, et al. Lancet Diabetes Endocrinol. 2019;7(5):344-355, PMID 30852132.) https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/29221645/

dose_response.reference_baseline: weight-stable recent-onset T2DM (<6 yr, non-insulin); DiRECT weight-stable/gain stratum ~0-3% remission taken as RR 1.0 of remaining diabetic
dose_response.baseline_dependence: Remission probability rises steeply with kg lost from weight-stable anchor: ~7% at 0-5 kg, ~34% at 5-10 kg, ~57% at 10-15 kg, ~86% at >=15 kg (DiRECT 12-mo). Steepest in 5-15 kg band, flat below ~5 kg, plateau above ~15 kg. Strongly dependent on diabetes duration/beta-cell reserve: near-zero credit in long-standing or insulin-dependent T2DM regardless of kg lost, since mechanism is unloading ectopic pancreatic/hepatic fat while beta-cell function is recoverable.

actions.target_outcome: incident type-2 diabetes
actions.source: Smith AD, Crippa A, Woodcock J, Brage S. Physical activity and incident type 2 diabetes mellitus: a systematic review and dose-response meta-analysis of prospective cohort studies. Diabetologia. 2016;59(12):2527-2545. (per-unit dose-response); Aune D, Norat T, Leitzmann M, Tonstad S, Vatten LJ. Physical activity and the risk of type 2 diabetes: a systematic review and dose-response meta-analysis. Eur J Epidemiol. 2015;30(7):529-542. (whole-dose scalar + nonlinearity) https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC6207340/

dose_response.unit: per +10 MET-hours/week of leisure-time physical activity
dose_response.baseline_dependence: Curvilinear/non-linear, steepest from sedentary; per-MET-h benefit diminishes as cumulative volume rises (most risk reduction accrued by ~5-7 h/week). Smith 2016 reports ~26% reduction at 11.25 MET-h/wk (RR ~0.74), ~36% at 22.5 MET-h/wk, ~53% at 60 MET-h/wk. No firm plateau; benefit continues above the 150 min/wk guideline.

actions.target_outcome: incident type-2 diabetes (Mediterranean dietary-pattern lever, metabolic slice of the mediterranean_diet bundle)
actions.source: Martinez-Gonzalez MA, et al. Yearly attained adherence to Mediterranean diet and incidence of diabetes in a large randomized trial. Cardiovascular Diabetology. 2023;22:262. (PREDIMED, per +2 MEDAS points HR 0.80 [0.70-0.92], strong inverse linear trend p=0.001; top 12-14 vs bottom <8 HR 0.46 [0.25-0.83]). Whole-dose scalar from PREDIMED diabetes substudy: Salas-Salvado J, et al. Prevention of diabetes with Mediterranean diets: a subgroup analysis of a randomized trial. Ann Intern Med. 2014;160(1):1-10 (EVOO arm HR 0.60 [0.43-0.85], nuts arm HR 0.82 [0.61-1.10]; bundle scalar set to 0.74 matching dietary_pattern_med). https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/37775736/

dose_response.unit: per +2 MEDAS adherence points (Mediterranean Diet Adherence Screener, 0-14 scale)
dose_response.baseline_dependence: steepest moving off a low-adherence Western baseline (MEDAS <8); incremental benefit flattens at high adherence (MEDAS >12) as the curve approaches its plateau, so the same +2 points buys less near the top of the scale

actions.target_outcome: incident activity-sensitive cancers (colorectal/colon, postmenopausal breast, endometrial; basket also includes kidney, liver, NHL)
actions.source: Moore SC, Lee IM, Weiderpass E, et al. Association of Leisure-Time Physical Activity With Risk of 26 Types of Cancer in 1.44 Million Adults. JAMA Intern Med. 2016;176(6):816-825. (90th-vs-10th-percentile HRs: colon 0.84, postmenopausal breast 0.90, endometrial 0.79; associations predominantly linear, cohort median 8 MET-hr/wk). Per-unit dose-response anchored to Schmid & Behrens, leisure-time PA and endometrial cancer dose-response meta (Eur J Epidemiol 2014, PMID 24375149): RR 0.98 per +3 MET-hr/wk ~= 0.993 per +1; basket ceiling and convex low-end steepness corroborated by Garcia et al, non-occupational PA dose-response meta, BJSM 2023, PMID 37316206 (5-16% lower cancer risk at 8.75 mMET-hr/wk). OBSERVATIONAL pooled cohorts -> moderate. https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC5812009/

dose_response.reference_baseline: sedentary / near-zero leisure-time activity (Moore 2016 10th-percentile reference group; Garcia 2023 uses 0 mMET-hr/wk as referent)
dose_response.baseline_dependence: Convex and steepest from sedentary: most achievable risk reduction is captured by ~8.75 marginal-MET-hr/wk (150 min/wk minimum; Garcia BJSM 2023 PMID 36854652: total cancer incidence RR ~0.88 / -12%, cancer mortality RR ~0.85 / -15% at this point), with markedly diminishing marginal benefit beyond ~15-17.5 MET-hr/wk. Per-unit credit applies to leisure/non-occupational volume above a near-sedentary baseline and should not be linearly extrapolated into the already-active range.

actions.target_outcome: incident diet-sensitive cancers (colorectal, breast, and the overall diet-sensitive basket) via a higher-quality Mediterranean dietary pattern
actions.source: Zhong Y, Zhu Y, Li Q, Wang F, Ge X, Zhou G, Miao L. Association between Mediterranean diet adherence and colorectal cancer: a dose-response meta-analysis. Am J Clin Nutr. 2020;111(6):1214-1225. https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/32359135/

dose_response.reference_baseline: lowest-adherence Western/poor-quality diet (index score ~0-2 of the scale)
dose_response.baseline_dependence: steepest moving off a poor-quality Western baseline; relative benefit flattens at high adherence (above roughly the upper tertile of the index), so the last points buy little additional risk reduction

actions.target_outcome: CV mortality (and all-cause mortality), incident type-2 diabetes, and colorectal cancer incidence -- dietary-fiber / whole-grain intake component lever; bundle slice of mediterranean_diet
actions.source: Reynolds A, Mann J, Cummings J, Winter N, Mete E, Te Morenga L. Carbohydrate quality and human health: a series of systematic reviews and meta-analyses. Lancet. 2019;393(10170):434-445. https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/30638909/

dose_response.reference_baseline: low habitual intake (~15 g/day, typical Western diet); curve anchored at the lowest-intake category in the pooled cohorts
dose_response.baseline_dependence: Steepest from low intake (~15 g/day); each +8 g/day buys ~5-27% lower risk across the outcome basket (all-cause mortality RR 0.93, CHD RR 0.81, incident T2DM RR 0.85, colorectal cancer within band). Risk reduction is greatest as intake rises toward 25-29 g/day, then the curve flattens with little additional credit beyond ~30 g/day.

actions.target_outcome: colorectal cancer incidence (single-booked primary outcome), with concordant reductions in incident T2DM and CHD/all-cause mortality, from reducing processed/red-meat intake
actions.source: Chan DSM, Lau R, Aune D, et al. Red and processed meat and colorectal cancer incidence: meta-analysis of prospective studies. PLoS One. 2011;6(6):e20456. Processed meat RR 1.18 (95% CI 1.10-1.28) per 50 g/day; red meat RR 1.17 (95% CI 1.05-1.31) per 100 g/day. Concordant multi-domain mortality signal: Pan A, et al. Red meat consumption and mortality. Arch Intern Med. 2012;172(7):555-63 (PMID 22412075), total-mortality HR 1.20 per serving/day processed, 1.13 unprocessed. Causal carcinogenicity classification: IARC Monograph 114 (processed meat Group 1, red meat Group 2A). Certainty downgraded per NutriRECS/Zeraatkar (Johnston BC, et al. Ann Intern Med. 2019, low-certainty evidence). https://journals.plos.org/plosone/article?id=10.1371/journal.pone.0020456

dose_response.reference_baseline: habitual high-consumer intake (~50-100+ g/day processed meat, upper tertile of Western diets); curve measured downward toward near-zero processed-meat intake
dose_response.baseline_dependence: Credit concentrated in high-consumers: log-linear per-50 g/day RR compounds from a high baseline, so the first ~50 g/day cut yields the most absolute benefit. Approximately linear up to ~140 g/day total red+processed intake, then plateaus (confirmed by WCRF-AICR CUP nonlinear analysis). Little to no credit below ~20 g/day processed meat. Red (unprocessed) meat carries a weaker per-unit signal (RR ~0.85 per -100 g/day, ~half the per-gram slope of processed meat), so processed-meat reductions dominate the curve.

actions.target_outcome: incident all-cause dementia (and Alzheimer's disease) via the leisure-time physical-activity volume lever
actions.source: Xu W, Wang HF, Wan Y, et al. Leisure time physical activity and dementia risk: a dose-response meta-analysis of prospective studies. BMJ Open. 2017;7(10):e014706. https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/29061599/

dose_response.reference_baseline: sedentary / lowest activity category (~0 MET-h/wk leisure-time activity)
dose_response.baseline_dependence: Steepest moving off sedentary; the largest absolute risk reduction comes from the first ~10-15 MET-h/wk (roughly 150 min/wk brisk walking). Xu 2017 reported a statistically LINEAR fit over the observed 0-45 MET-h/wk range (p-nonlinearity 0.87 for ACD), so incremental per-10-MET-h credit is constant within that range on the additive scale; the encoded flattening toward the RR 0.80 ceiling is an interpretive plateau drawn from the high-vs-low pooled estimate (Iso-Markku 2022), not from a measured nonlinear term in Xu.

actions.target_outcome: global cognition / cognitive decline (NTB composite, a real cognitive outcome) in at-risk older adults, including the genetically elevated-risk slice
actions.source: Ngandu T, et al. A 2 year multidomain intervention of diet, exercise, cognitive training, and vascular risk monitoring versus control to prevent cognitive decline in at-risk elderly people (FINGER): a randomised controlled trial. Lancet. 2015;385(9984):2255-63. Dose-response anchored to Ngandu T, et al. The effect of adherence on cognition in a multidomain lifestyle intervention (FINGER). Alzheimers Dement. 2022;18(7):1325-1334 (PMID 34668644). https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/25771249/

dose_response.unit: per +1 multidomain lifestyle-score domain actively adopted (of the 4-domain FINGER protocol: structured diet, exercise, cognitive training, vascular-risk monitoring)
dose_response.reference_baseline: cognitively at-risk older adult (CAIDE-elevated / genetic-risk) at a poor-quality, low-engagement lifestyle baseline; 0 domains adopted
dose_response.baseline_dependence: steepest going from a poor-quality/sedentary baseline toward the protocol -- early domains adopted carry most of the benefit; the FINGER adherence analysis (PMID 34668644) found active participation and lifestyle-score improvement each tracked better NTB trajectories, and the trial conclusion is that the dose must be 'intensive enough', i.e. partial adoption underperforms; flattens once most of the protocol is adopted

actions.target_outcome: incident all-cause dementia in genetically high-risk individuals (high polygenic risk / APOE-e4 carriers)
actions.source: Lourida I, Hannon E, Littlejohns TJ, et al. Association of Lifestyle and Genetic Risk With Incidence of Dementia. JAMA. 2019;322(5):430-437. doi:10.1001/jama.2019.9879. UK Biobank, N=196,383, 1,769 dementia cases, 1,545,433 person-years (median 8.0y). In the high-genetic-risk (top-tertile PRS) stratum, favorable vs unfavorable lifestyle HR 0.68 (95% CI 0.51-0.90); no significant lifestyle x polygenic-risk interaction (P=0.99). Overall favorable vs unfavorable HR 0.65 (reciprocal of the reported unfavorable-vs-favorable HR 1.35, 95% CI 1.15-1.58); intermediate HR 1.17 (1.04-1.31), unfavorable HR 1.35 (1.15-1.58) vs favorable. https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC6628594/

dose_response.unit: per +1 favorable lifestyle factor (0-4 healthy-lifestyle score: no current smoking; regular physical activity >=150 min/wk moderate or >=75 min/wk vigorous; healthy diet >=4 of 7 food groups; moderate alcohol)
dose_response.reference_baseline: unfavorable lifestyle (0-1 favorable factors; Lourida weighted-score band 0-50) within the high genetic-risk (top-tertile PRS) stratum
dose_response.baseline_dependence: Multiplicative per factor, so absolute hazard reduction is largest moving off the unfavorable end and shrinks/flattens as factors accumulate into the favorable band (3-4 factors). No lifestyle x genetic-risk interaction was detected (P=0.99), so the per-factor slope is treated as constant across genetic-risk strata; however, the high-dose credit is least certain in APOE-e4 homozygotes, where multiple cohorts (e.g. Hisayama/Japanese aging cohorts) report attenuated or absent benefit. The per-unit HR is a back-derivation (0.68^(1/3)~=0.88, ~12% lower hazard per factor) from the categorical favorable-vs-unfavorable contrast spanning ~3 factors, not a directly published per-factor HR; the paper reports a continuous trend (P<0.001) consistent with this.

actions.target_outcome: motor symptom progression (MDS-UPDRS-III / UPDRS-III) in early, mild Parkinson disease (Hoehn & Yahr <=2, within ~5 yr of diagnosis)
actions.source: Schenkman M, Moore CG, Kohrt WM, et al. Effect of High-Intensity Treadmill Exercise on Motor Symptoms in Patients With De Novo Parkinson Disease (SPARX): A Phase 2 Randomized Clinical Trial. JAMA Neurol. 2018;75(2):219-226. https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC5838616/

dose_response.reference_baseline: sedentary / no structured aerobic exercise (and moderate-intensity 60-65% HRmax, which behaved like baseline for motor progression in SPARX)
dose_response.baseline_dependence: Sharp intensity threshold, not a smooth gradient. Grounded qualitatively: in SPARX (PMID 29228079) the 60-65% HRmax moderate arm did NOT reach the prespecified nonfutility threshold, while the 80-85% HRmax high-intensity arm (4 d/wk) did. Benefit is concentrated above ~75-80% HRmax; volume/frequency below the high-intensity band buys little motor benefit -- intensity, not minutes, is the lever. CAVEAT: per_unit_effect 0.93 per +5% HRmax is an editorial interpolation, NOT a measured slope -- SPARX tested only two discrete intensity doses (a binary contrast, one of them null), so no continuous per-5%-HRmax gradient was estimated. Treat the per-unit number as an order-of-magnitude placeholder; the defensible content is the threshold shape and the ceiling, not the slope. Encodes potential IF the high-intensity dose is achieved (no adherence/achievability discount applied). [per-unit is an editorial interpolation, not a measured slope; treat threshold/ceiling as primary]

actions.target_outcome: incident all-cause dementia (adjudicated) over 10-year follow-up in initially cognitively healthy older adults
actions.source: Edwards JD, Xu H, Clark DO, Guey LT, Ross LA, Unverzagt FW. Speed of processing training results in lower risk of dementia. Alzheimers Dement (N Y). 2017;3(4):603-611. (ACTIVE trial, NCT00298558) https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC5700828/

dose_response.reference_baseline: zero speed-of-processing training sessions (no-contact control); ACTIVE delivered ~10 initial sessions plus up to ~8 booster sessions over 35 months
dose_response.baseline_dependence: Multiplicative per-session hazard, so absolute risk reduction is steepest at the low end (the first ~10 initial sessions carry most of the effect) and flattens as cumulative dose accumulates; benefit credited only to the speed-of-processing modality, not the memory or reasoning arms (both HR ~0.79, non-significant). NOTE: the per-session HR 0.90 is the UNADJUSTED estimate (95% CI 0.85-0.95, P<.001); confounding by adherence/engagement cannot be excluded, so the curve is indicative not causal at the per-session level.
